narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 36, middle family income bracket, Year 1 grade decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 6, and plans to attend full-time—solid details that paint a picture of your journey so far. The admissions model, trained on thousands of similar students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 60% chance you'll fail it on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your undergraduate GPA, which sits notably below the typical 3.21 average and strongly signaled higher risk in the model's eyes. Next in influence, your Year 3 grade decile of 6—slightly above the average of 5.53—worked in your favor, nudging the odds toward passing by showing improvement over time. Your middle family income also contributed toward the failure prediction, as it aligned with profiles where students faced extra hurdles compared to higher-income peers. While your LSAT was close to the 36.70 average and full-time status is the norm for 93% of students, those didn't outweigh the top concerns. In essence, your GPA stood out as the heaviest anchor, even as your later school performance offered some counterbalance. Compared to students who typically pass, your profile mirrors many who struggle with early academic metrics despite decent test scores. This rejection stings, especially given the barriers Hispanic students like you often navigate, but it's one data point—not your destiny. To move forward, consider retaking undergrad-level courses or targeted GPA-boosting programs to lift that key metric, while building on your Year 3 momentum through internships or bar prep simulations. Many in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps, and law schools often revisit strong follow-up applications. Keep pushing; your story has room to shift the odds.
